old_claim.note: Computerized Tomography (CT/MRI) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Electron tomography reconstructs three-dimensional volumes from projection images, so computational reconstruction algorithms enable advanced electron microscopy.

source_shape.source_locator: PubMed abstract for a review of computational methods in electron tomography.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The review describes electron tomography as a workflow where projection images are processed and combined into a three-dimensional tomogram.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports computation theory as the relevant enabler because the source focuses on reconstruction algorithms for electron microscopy data, not on medical CT or MRI as prerequisites.

ontology_before: Advanced electron microscopy depended on a medical CT/MRI bundle, conflating a reconstruction idea with unrelated clinical imaging modalities.
ontology_after: Advanced electron microscopy keeps electron microscope and DSP foundations while using computation theory for tomographic reconstruction.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the false medical-imaging bundle edge is absent. Preserved: computational reconstruction remains represented.
would_reject_if: A source showed advanced electron microscopy requires medical CT or MRI hardware as a method prerequisite.
The graph reintroduced computerized_tomography_ct_mri as a direct advanced electron microscopy prerequisite.
The replacement edge were changed to a non-computational application node.
short_reason: Electron microscopy tomography needs reconstruction algorithms, not medical CT/MRI technology.
